[2018-01-29 11:48] mtanzer: I have some things to add on the topic of "inflation"
[2018-01-29 11:48] mtanzer: The first point I want to make is you absolutely don't need to do this kind of math to be good at prismata.  I've done all of this math myself and it has helped me learn some things, but any of the things I've learned I can share with people who don't want to do this math themselves anyway.  A recent example is on apooche's stream yesterday we were talking about which of centurion or defense grid is usually better.  We did some math and worked out that centurion is usually significantly better and a lot of the difference comes from the fact that it's prompt and dgrid isn't.  But even without knowing the math behind it other players can still just take our word for this, or maybe figure it out from gameplay if they've seen enough games where one player got cent and the other got dgrid.
[2018-01-29 11:48] mtanzer: However I do find this "inflation" math is pretty useful in examining relatively close/small decisions like whether to click drake to kill a rhino or whether it's worth buying 4 more defense to keep an extra (non-absorbing) xeno guardian alive.  some of these closer decisions probably won't change the outcome of so many games, so it's hard to work out how good/bad they are just from experience.  Also complicated things like whether to absorb on xaetron every turn, every other turn, or every third turn, or even if you want xae at all when there's some other absorber like infusion grid or energy matrix.  The math can give me a rule of thumb for many of these types of decisions (almost always both/all options can be correct depending on the exact situation, but there tends to be one that's correct more often).
[2018-01-29 11:48] mtanzer: Lastly I want to mention I'm still not entirely sure why people use the word "inflation" instead of "interest rate" or "ROI."  Inflation refers to things increasing in cost over time.  An effect of this is the same amount of money in the future can buy less stuff than it can now because things cost more.  This effect exists in prismata, but I've never really felt like increased cost was the cause.  For example, having 3 gold in one turn is often worth 1 gold less than having it now because you can buy drone this turn and have an extra gold next turn, but it's not like the cost of drone actually increased from this turn to the next.  It's just that by not investing your 3 gold on the current turn, you miss out on getting that return on investment of 1 gold/turn.  The cost of individual units never actually increases.
[2018-01-29 11:49] mtanzer: I guess a case one could make for "inflation" is the cost of soak increases over time because earlier in the game you're buying the better soak like walls and later on you're forced to use more expensive soak like rhinos or holding drones.  But this really just depends on how much attack the opponent has, how much soak you can buy in a single turn, and how many supplies are left.  The way I see it, the costs of the good and bad soak didn't change, only your ability to buy enough of the good soak.
[2018-01-29 11:49] mtanzer: that's all for now, sorry for all the inflation spam 😃  if anyone else has any thoughts on the topic I'm happy to discuss

[2018-01-29 21:21] .jrkirby (jrkirby): It's actually impossible for shadowfang to be a good investment while animus is not. Animus's value depends on red being valuable. Shadowfang's cost depends on red's cost. If you think shadowfang is valuable but animus isn't - that's because you've placed the wrong value on red. You're undervaluing it, so shadowfang feels cheaper than it is (thus better ROI), and animus seems less valuable than it is (thus less ROI).
[2018-01-29 21:22] .jrkirby (jrkirby): If it's worth it to get shadowfang, it is implicitly worth it to get the animus first. If you can't that means it wasn't worth it.

[2018-01-29 21:27] apooche: @mtanzer inflation can refer to just an increase in money supply or decrease in buying power of money (or both). Early game money supply (gold and resources, including attack) increases. Lategame buying power goes down as turns buying efficient attack and defense become impossible and you resort to plays that give up long term gains to remain alive. I would contend that costs do go up in some cases. Wall gets a whole lot more expensive when you run out of supply and have to switch to less efficient defense.

[2018-01-29 21:55] mtanzer: @Apooche hmm I always thought the decrease in buying power of money was a consequence of inflation, not the inflation itself, although it's possible I'm just wrong.  I admit that the interpretation that the cost of soak increases as the game goes on is what I would refer to as inflation.  I think the reason it doesn't feel like it fits to me is in a real economy when inflation occurs, you have to pay more currency to get the exact same thing.  I think of the different units as being distinct, so for example if you compare holding 3 drones for 3 soak to buying 3 barriers (even ignoring lifespan), yes the drones are more expensive but I've always thought of that as a different purchase.  So if you think of holding 3 drones and buying 3 barriers as different ways of purchasing the same thing then I get why the term inflation would apply.

[2018-01-30 19:41] 307th: because the top players will still have a small cluster of ways they'd play a set, all of which are good, whereas less good players will be way outside that small cluster
[2018-01-30 19:41] 307th: so, like, if A, B, and C are legitimate ways to play a set, it's fine to make a guide pushing for "play this set like B"
[2018-01-30 19:42] 307th: especially if the alternative is them playing in way H
[2018-01-30 19:42] redrame: There are a lot of interesting guidelines that I never see people mention when set reading
[2018-01-30 19:42] redrame: For example, “if you’re gonna buy animus, buy it before blastforge”
[2018-01-30 19:43] redrame: I think that guideline is pretty useful in making openings for new players
[2018-01-30 19:43] msven: i dunno, there's lots of sets where i prefer buying blastforge before animus when i'm getting both
[2018-01-30 19:43] redrame: Yeah, it sucks to always be wrong
[2018-01-30 19:44] 307th: a more accurate version is "make an animus when it's time to make attackers, make a blastforge when it's time to make defense; since tarsiers take 2 turns to build, if you and your opponent are playing the set in a simliar way, it's better to buy animus before blastforge"
[2018-01-30 19:44] redrame: Yeah that ones pretty good
[2018-01-30 19:44] 307th: i'm sure there's issues with that too though
[2018-01-30 19:45] redrame: I like the 307th guide more than the lunarch guide rn for sure though
[2018-01-30 19:45] redrame: Lunarch guide feels kinda forced
[2018-01-30 19:46] redrame: Like 2 nights ago I wrote about “rushing” and I’m not even really sure what is meant by rushing in this game
[2018-01-30 19:46] redrame: Kind of a weird concept to apply to Prismata since you never have the option to breach early unless opp is greedy
[2018-01-30 19:47] 307th: it kind of applies to ideas like when one person goes dgrid and other person goes electrovore
[2018-01-30 19:47] 307th: but yeah it's pretty rare
[2018-01-30 19:47] 307th: guides are just crazy hard to write
[2018-01-30 19:48] redrame: Yeah, rushes happen occasionally, but teaching about them is pointless
[2018-01-30 19:48] 307th: when i started  making guides i was thinking "most games don't even have a good set of guides for beginners, so if Prismata has some, it should have a big advantage"
[2018-01-30 19:48] redrame: Instead I just kind of made it initiative part II
[2018-01-30 19:48] 307th: but now i realize why even really big games don't have super well done guides
[2018-01-30 19:48] 307th: it's just really difficult
[2018-01-30 19:48] redrame: To be fair I think my section on rushing is better than the initiative section
[2018-01-30 19:49] redrame: But like they don’t need to both exist
[2018-01-30 19:49] 307th: i think good guides can make a big difference though
[2018-01-30 19:50] 307th: like, i never got into RTS's as a kid
[2018-01-30 19:50] 307th: because i never really 'got' how to play them competitively
[2018-01-30 19:50] supergreyman (Greyman): One of the things that makes Prismata guide harder to write about is the fact that every set is different. It's not like Starcraft where each set of units is always the same.
[2018-01-30 19:50] 307th: but when i tried as a late-teenager, a day9 video made it click for me
[2018-01-30 19:50] 307th: just basic stuff about always making workers and keeping resources low
[2018-01-30 19:51] 307th: but that basic stuff hadn't really been taught in, say, Warcraft 3
[2018-01-30 19:51] 307th: or brood war, when i played it as a kid
[2018-01-30 19:52] supergreyman (Greyman): Maybe a video guide is much better than written guide?
[2018-01-30 19:52] 307th: well, there's two different types of video guides
[2018-01-30 19:52] 307th: i should say "video strategy content"
[2018-01-30 19:53] 307th: one is basically streaming
[2018-01-30 19:53] 307th: you play games with commentary
[2018-01-30 19:53] 307th: it's easy to do, and is a good way to learn
[2018-01-30 19:53] 307th: but it's bad at teaching fundamental concepts
[2018-01-30 19:53] redrame: I am definitely gonna emphasize reading sets and playing reactively more than anything else
[2018-01-30 19:53] 307th: and people generally have to watch for hours to learn
[2018-01-30 19:53] redrame: I think it needs more than a synopsis
[2018-01-30 19:54] 307th: another is guides like day9 did, where he planned it and then did it on a regular basis
[2018-01-30 19:54] 307th: but he would still do guides that were easy to do a lot of; like watching a game between two players and talking about it
[2018-01-30 19:54] 307th: a third would be 100% scripted videos
[2018-01-30 19:54] 307th: that would be much shorter and aimed at teaching specific concepts
[2018-01-30 19:55] 307th: that would be like if i just read one of my blog posts out loud with a slide show
[2018-01-30 19:55] 307th: if i'm going to do the third type, it seems easier to just write the blog post and post it
[2018-01-30 19:55] 307th: the reading it out loud just adds another dimension i can screw up

messages.content: One of the things that makes Prismata guide harder to write about is the fact that every set is different. It's not like Starcraft where each set of units is always the same.

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: but when i tried as a late-teenager, a day9 video made it click for me

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: just basic stuff about always making workers and keeping resources low

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: but that basic stuff hadn't really been taught in, say, Warcraft 3

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: or brood war, when i played it as a kid

messages.author: supergreyman
messages.content: Maybe a video guide is much better than written guide?

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: well, there's two different types of video guides

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: i should say "video strategy content"

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: one is basically streaming

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: you play games with commentary

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: it's easy to do, and is a good way to learn

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: but it's bad at teaching fundamental concepts

messages.author: redrame
messages.content: I am definitely gonna emphasize reading sets and playing reactively more than anything else

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: and people generally have to watch for hours to learn

messages.author: redrame
messages.content: I think it needs more than a synopsis

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: another is guides like day9 did, where he planned it and then did it on a regular basis

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: but he would still do guides that were easy to do a lot of; like watching a game between two players and talking about it

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: a third would be 100% scripted videos

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: that would be much shorter and aimed at teaching specific concepts

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: that would be like if i just read one of my blog posts out loud with a slide show

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: if i'm going to do the third type, it seems easier to just write the blog post and post it

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: the reading it out loud just adds another dimension i can screw up
